planting.note: Scarify seeds before direct sowing in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; large-fruited wild indigo is a southeastern native adapted to bottomland forests and moist, shaded stream margins.

directions: Scarify seeds (nick the hard seed coat with a file or soak in hot water overnight) before direct sowing in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; large-fruited wild indigo is adapted to moist, shaded bottomland forests and differs from most baptisias in its shade tolerance. Baptisia megacarpa is a southeastern US native wild indigo of moist, shaded bottomlands and stream margins in the Florida Panhandle and adjacent Georgia and Alabama; it produces lavender to white, pea-like flowers in spring and very large, inflated, oval seed pods (the largest of any baptisia) that rattle when dry — outstanding as an ornamental dried pod. Unlike the sun-loving prairie baptisias (B. australis, B. bracteata), this species adapts to partial shade and moist soil, making it unusual and valuable in the genus for woodland garden use in zones 7–10.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; hyssopleaf thoroughwort is exceptionally drought-tolerant and adapts to poor, dry, sandy soils.

directions: Sow direct in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; hyssopleaf thoroughwort is one of the most drought-tolerant native eupatoriums and thrives in sandy, dry, open woodlands and roadsides in the Southeast. Eupatorium hyssopifolium is an eastern US native perennial with linear, narrow (hyssop-like) leaves arranged in whorls on slender stems and flat-topped clusters of small, white thoroughwort flowers in late summer through fall; it differs from the common boneset (E. perfoliatum) primarily in its narrow leaves and greater drought tolerance. Like all eupatoriums, it is an outstanding late-season nectar plant for monarch butterflies, native bees, and wasps; the small, white flowers attract an enormous diversity of pollinator species in late summer. Excellent for dry, sunny native plant borders and sandy meadow restorations in the Southeast.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall for cold stratification and spring germination in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; gray beardtongue is one of the most drought-tolerant native penstemons of the Appalachian region.

directions: Sow direct in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; gray beardtongue tolerates dry, rocky, thin soils better than most native penstemons and does not need rich soil to perform. Penstemon canescens is a native penstemon of the Appalachian region from Pennsylvania to Georgia, growing in dry, rocky woods, slopes, and ledges; it has grayish-hairy (canescent) leaves and produces loose, terminal racemes of tubular, lavender to pink-purple flowers with darker veining in late spring and early summer that are visited by native bumble bees, large carpenter bees, and hummingbirds. The soft gray foliage is ornamental even when not in bloom; it naturalizes in rocky, dry-slope native plant gardens and is excellent in combination with native grasses and drought-tolerant forbs.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Arkansas beardtongue is a small, slender native penstemon of the south-central US excellent for limestone glades, rocky prairies, and sunny native plant gardens in zones 5–9.

directions: Sow direct in fall for cold stratification and spring germination in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, calcareous or rocky soil in full sun; Arkansas beardtongue requires excellent drainage and is adapted to the thin, rocky, alkaline limestone glades and prairies of the Ozarks and Ouachitas. Penstemon arkansanus is a small, delicate native penstemon of rocky, limestone glades, cedars barrens, and rocky prairies in Arkansas, Missouri, Oklahoma, and adjacent states; it produces narrow, linear leaves and slender spikes of small, white to pale-lavender, tubular flowers with a slightly two-lipped structure from April through June. Though small and often overlooked, it is a tough, adaptable native perennial for dry, calcareous soils in zones 5–9 where it naturalizes and self-seeds in favorable, well-drained spots; it is an excellent early-season native bee plant.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; rosilla is a California and Oregon native sneezeweed adapted to moist, seasonally flooded grasslands and stream margins.

directions: Sow direct in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; rosilla is a wetland-affiliated native that thrives in consistently moist conditions near streams, ponds, and in rain gardens — it is poorly adapted to dry soils. Helenium puberulum is a California and Oregon native sneezeweed of moist grasslands, stream margins, and valley bottoms; it produces winged stems with decurrent leaf bases (a distinguishing feature of helenium) and yellow, daisy-like flowers with reflexed ray petals around a prominent, rounded yellow to greenish disk in summer through fall. Like all heleniums, it is an excellent late-season pollinator plant, providing nectar for a broad range of native bees, butterflies, and wasps; the yellow flowers are intensely attractive to bumblebees. It naturalizes readily in moist, sunny California-native gardens and is effective in mass plantings at pond margins and in wet meadow restorations.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; green gentian is monocarpic — it may live 20–80 years as a vegetative rosette before flowering once magnificently, then dying.

directions: Sow direct in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; green gentian is monocarpic (flowers once, then dies) after an extraordinary lifespan as a vegetative rosette of 20–80 years — plant several seedlings to ensure flowering plants at different times. Frasera speciosa is one of the most botanically fascinating native plants of the Rocky Mountain region — it grows as a flat rosette of large, strap-shaped, silvery-veined leaves for many years (sometimes decades), then when conditions are right it sends up an impressive, 3–7-foot-tall flowering spike bearing hundreds of 4-petaled, greenish-white to pale-yellow flowers with purple spots and fringed nectary glands in a spectacular, cathedral-like inflorescence before dying. The ecological synchrony (mass flowering events in some populations) is remarkable; the plant is an important bear food and native bee resource when it blooms. It is an extraordinary garden plant for patient native plant enthusiasts.
